summary: DHH argues that Linux's low adoption rate among general users is analogous to why people don't exercise or read more — it requires effort that most people avoid. He acknowledges that casual computer users have no obligation to use Linux, but contends that programmers are different. Like firefighters who maintain physical fitness as a prerequisite for their job, developers should embrace Linux as a training ground for deeper computing proficiency. He points to his Omakub project as a way for developers to give Linux another look.
key_points: Linux's low adoption follows the same pattern as other beneficial-but-effortful activities like exercise and reading — most people choose the path of least resistance
Linux serves as a 'dojo' that forces users to develop deeper understanding of computing fundamentals through hands-on problem solving
Programmers have a professional obligation to develop computing proficiency that casual users don't, making Linux a worthwhile investment for them
Developers who can navigate JavaScript build pipelines or AWS IAM already have the technical stamina needed for Linux
money_quotes: The world is full of free invitations to self-improvement that are ignored by most people most of the time.
Putting it crudely, it's easier to be fat and ignorant in a world of cheap, empty calories than it is to be fit and informed.
Linux isn't minimal effort. It's an operating system that demands more of you than does the commercial offerings from Microsoft and Apple.
It serves as a dojo for understanding computers better. With a sensei who keeps demanding you figure problems out on your own in order to learn and level up.
They're like firefighters. Fitness isn't the purpose of firefighting, but a prerequisite.
If you're able to figure out how to setup a modern build pipeline for JavaScript or even correctly configure IAM for AWS, you already have all the stamina you need for the Linux journey.
Not because it is easy, but because it is worth it.
key_insight: Programmers should use Linux not because it's easy or convenient, but because the effort of mastering it builds foundational computing skills that make them better at their craft.
